perturbation.original_text: I will not at any time, whether during or after the termination of employment, for any reason whatsoever (other than to promote and advance the business of the Company), reveal to any person or entity (both commercial and non-commercial) or use for any purpose other than the furtherance of the Company's business interests any of the trade secrets or Confidential Information, including, but not limited to, the Company's research and development activities, marketing plans and strategies, pricing and costing policies, customer and supplier lists, and business or financial information of the Company so far as they have come or may come to my knowledge, except as may be required in the ordinary course of performing my duties as an employee of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: I will endeavor to not at any time, whether during or after the termination of employment, reveal to any person or entity or use for any purpose other than the furtherance of the Company's business interests any of the trade secrets or Confidential Information, including, but not limited to, the Company's research and development activities, marketing plans and strategies, pricing and costing policies, customer and supplier lists, and business or financial information of the Company so far as they have come or may come to my knowledge, except as may be required in the ordinary course of performing my duties as an employee of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'I will not' to 'I will endeavor to not' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. 'Endeavor' implies an attempt rather than a guarantee of non-disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act prohibits the misappropriation of trade secrets. Changing 'will not' to 'endeavor to not' creates ambiguity about the employee's responsibility, potentially conflicting with the Act's intention to strictly protect trade secrets.
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'I will not' to 'I will endeavor to not' introduces ambiguity regarding the employee's obligation to protect confidential information. The Economic Espionage Act, as referenced by the LLM Law Explanation and Snippet 1 (Economic Espionage Act of 1996), intends to protect trade secrets strictly. The changed text weakens this protection, creating a contradiction because 'endeavor to not' suggests an attempt rather than a firm commitment, potentially undermining the Act's objective.

perturbation.original_text: I hereby assign to the Company the sole and exclusive right to such Inventions. I agree that I will promptly disclose to the Company any and all such Inventions, and that, upon request of the Company, I will execute and deliver any and all documents or instruments and take any other action which the Company shall deem necessary to assign to and vest completely in the Company, to perfect trademark, copyright and patent protection with respect to, or to otherwise protect the Company's trade secrets and proprietary interest in such Inventions.
perturbation.changed_text: I hereby assign to the Company the right to such Inventions. I agree that I will disclose to the Company such Inventions, and that, upon a reasonable request of the Company, I will execute and deliver documents or instruments and take action which the Company shall deem necessary to assign to the Company, to protect the Company's interest in such Inventions.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'sole and exclusive' from 'the sole and exclusive right' makes the assignment less definite. Replacing 'promptly' with the removal of any adjective, and inserting 'a reasonable request' introduces subjectivity. Also removing 'completely' and 'perfect trademark, copyright and patent protection with respect to, or to otherwise perfect' weakens obligation and enforcement, allowing for a narrower interpretation of 'protect'.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Patent Act specifies conditions for patent assignment. By removing "sole and exclusive right" and making the disclosure and execution of documents subject to 'reasonableness', the clause now ambiguously addresses the assignment of rights, which can hinder the company's ability to secure patent protection, conflicting with the intent of the Patent Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment U.S. Code Notes prev | next Subject to the provisions of this title, patents shall have the attributes of personal property. The Patent and Trademark Office shall maintain a register of interests in patents and applications for patents and shall record any document related thereto upon request, and may require a fee therefor. Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing. The applicant, patentee, or his assigns or legal representatives may in like manner grant and convey an exclusive right under his application for patent, or patents, to the whole or any specified part of the United States. A certificate of acknowledgment under the hand and official seal of a person authorized to administer oaths within the United States, or, in a foreign country, of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States or an officer authorized to administer oaths whose authority is proved by a certificate of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States, or apostille of an official designated by a foreign country which, by treaty or convention, accords like effect to apostilles of designated officials in the United States, shall be prima facie evidence of the execution of an assignment, grant or conveyance of a patent or application for patent. An interest that constitutes an assignment, grant or conveyance shall be void as against any subsequent purchaser or mortgagee for a valuable consideration, without notice, unless it is recorded in the Patent and Trademark Office within three months from its date or prior to the date of such subsequent purchase or mortgage. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 810 ; Pub. L. 93–596, § 1 , Jan. 2, 1975 , 88 Stat. 1949 ; Pub. L. 97–247, § 14(b) , Aug. 27, 1982
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities that undermine the company's ability to secure patent protection, potentially conflicting with the intent of the Patent Act, as the original text provides a more definite assignment of rights. The removal of 'sole and exclusive right' and the introduction of 'reasonableness' create a vagueness around the assignment and the required actions. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing'. The changes weaken the enforceability and clarity of this assignment, making it potentially more difficult to protect the Company's interests, and thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: While I am employed by the Company and for a period of two (2) years after the termination or cessation of such employment for any reason, I shall not, without the Company's prior written consent, directly or indirectly, alone or as a partner, joint venturer, officer, director, employee, consultant, agent, independent contractor or stockholder of any company or business, engage in any business activity which is or may reasonably be construed to be competitive with the "PathNet Business."
perturbation.changed_text: While I am employed by the Company and for a period of two (2) years after the termination or cessation of such employment, I should not, without the Company's consent, engage in any business activity that might be seen as possibly competitive with the "PathNet Business."
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'should not' weakens the non-competition covenant. Replacing 'prior written consent' with just 'consent' removes clarity on the form of consent needed. Modifying 'which is or may reasonably be construed to be' to 'that might be seen as possibly competitive' further weakens the restriction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 188 addresses the enforceability of agreements in restraint of trade. By weakening the non-compete clause, the modified text makes it more difficult for the company to demonstrate that the restrictions are reasonable and necessary to protect its legitimate business interests, thus contradicting the Restatement's principles on balancing competitive interests with individual freedom to contract.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall not' to 'should not' weakens the non-compete clause, and the altered consent requirement introduces ambiguity. The law explanation highlights that these changes make it harder for the company to prove the restrictions are reasonable and necessary, potentially contradicting principles balancing competitive interests. Given the absence of law snippets, the contradiction determination is based on the analysis provided in the perturbation and law explanations, and the direct weakening of the obligations in the original text compared to the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: During my employment with the Company and for a period two (2) years thereafter, I will not encourage any employee of the Company to terminate their employment with the Company, nor solicit nor hire any employee of the Company for employment by any corporation or any other commercial enterprise other than the Company, unless first approached in writing by the Company employee.
perturbation.changed_text: During my employment with the Company, I will try not to encourage any employee of the Company to terminate their employment with the Company, nor solicit nor hire any employee of the Company for employment by any other commercial enterprise, unless contacted by the Company employee.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'I will not' to 'I will try not to' makes the obligation to not encourage employees to leave less strict. Removing 'for a period two (2) years thereafter' shortens the non-solicitation period. Replacing 'any corporation or any other commercial enterprise other than the Company' with 'any other commercial enterprise' removes the explicit mention of corporations. Removing 'in writing' from the last clause takes away a concrete standard of permission.
perturbation.contradicted_law: District of Columbia Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The non-solicitation clause, intended to protect the company's workforce and prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets by ex-employees, is weakened. The alteration makes injunctive relief harder to get. The Act covers actions to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. Making the anti-solicitation clause less definitive makes it less effective at protecting against the misappropriation of trade secrets by competitors who might solicit the company's employees.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 36â402. Injunctive relief. | D.C. Law Library Â Toggle mobile menu Code of the District of Columbia Â§ 36â402. Injunctive relief. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for a reasonable period of time to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of a misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, an affirmative act to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. ( Mar. 16, 1989, D.C. Law 7-216, Â§ 3, 36 DCR 519 .) Prior Codifications 1981 Ed., Â§â48-502. Editor's Notes Uniform Law: This section is based upon Â§â2 of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'I will not' to 'I will try not to' weakens the obligation. Removing 'for a period two (2) years thereafter' shortens the non-solicitation period. These changes introduce ambiguity and reduce the enforceability of the non-solicitation agreement, especially concerning injunctive relief as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined'. The changes undermine the company's ability to protect its trade secrets by making it more difficult to obtain an injunction against former employees who might solicit current employees, thus creating a legal contradiction. The anti-solicitation clause is less definitive and therefore less effective.
